text: Good afternoon, everybody. Our patient is a fifty-five-year-old male patient. He has no basis of any chronic illness. He has a basis of appendectomy before. This patient has been diagnosed as ascending colon cancer, adenocarcinoma, T-three, M-positive, M-negative.

The assistant uses a grasper to hold tissue near the ileocecal valve, while the surgeon pushes towards the right iliac fossa. The ileocolic vessels are visible, indicated by the grasper, showing the site of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV), aligned with the level of the falciform ligament. The surgeon begins dissection under the ileocolic vessels, moving four centimeters below the vessel outbowing and four centimeters from the SMV.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to map the route to the root of the ileocolic vessel, aiming for centralized visualization and lymph node harvesting near the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). A tunnel is created, separating the mesorectum from retroperitoneal structures, ensuring the duodenum remains unharmed during dissection.

The surgeon uses a grasper to indicate the duodenum, highlighting the risk of injury if not properly dissected away. A gauze is placed inside the tunnel to protect the duodenum during dissection. The surgeon searches for the root of the ileocolic vessel, emphasizing the importance of complete lymph node removal to avoid leaving positive lymph node tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects the fat covering the superior mesenteric vein and the origin of the ileocolic vessel, ensuring adequate lymph node harvesting to prevent recurrence and minimize the need for chemotherapy.

The surgeon opens the flimsy membrane and dissects to separate the vessel, with gauze protecting the duodenum. They search for the ileocolic vein and artery, noting that the vein is typically more superficial. Small bleeding occurs, prompting meticulous hemostasis. An energy device is used to remove fat over the ileocolic vessel, aiming to visualize and clip it. The superior mesenteric vein is identified for ligation at the root. A lymph node is noted in the area, emphasizing the importance of proper central vessel ligation. Fat covering the vessel is removed using traction and various energy devices.

The surgeon identifies a bluish or whitish structure, likely the ileocolic vein, and begins to skeletonize it for clear clipping. Using the harmonic instrument, the surgeon dissects the posterior tissue, grasping and sealing the bloodless fatty tissue before cutting. The procedure focuses on central vessel ligation at the root.

The surgeon uses a right-angle instrument to separate the vein, clearly visualizing the vein and the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). Clips are applied precisely at the root of the vein, after removing all surrounding fatty tissue and lymph nodes. Two clips are placed for security, ensuring the vein is flush with the SMV. The surgeon uses an energy sealing device on the vein for hemostasis.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the ileocolic artery, using a right-angle instrument to create a window for encircling the artery. Clips are applied at the root of the vessel, ensuring there is no tension to prevent tearing and bleeding.

The surgeon carefully places two proximal clips on the ileocolic artery, ensuring they are positioned away from the vessel's origin. The superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and ileocolic vein are visible. Using scissors, the surgeon cuts the artery, revealing its lumen, while placing clips flush with the SMV.

Because if injury occurred during applying the first one, so we have still small stump which we can catch it by grasp bar and putting again clips. Or controlling by sealing device, whatever, we can control it sometime suturing it. Thank you very much for your observation.
This was Dr. Fauzan Siouri, colorectal surgeon.
